blocks.text: On September 1834, after Joseph Smith’s return from the Zion’s Camp march, Church leaders in Kirtland established a committee to arrange “items of the doctrine of Jesus Christ” into a volume to be known as the “book of covenants.” The committee members—Joseph Smith, Oliver Cowdery, Sidney Rigdon, and Frederick G. Williams—were to extract these items from the Bible, the Book of Mormon, and the revelations Joseph had received.

From November 1834 to March 1835, a School for the Elders operated in Kirtland, succeeding the School of the Prophets held the previous winter. Lectures prepared for religious instruction at the new school helped fulfill the assignment of the Book of Covenants committee to arrange items of doctrine.  Three of these lectures soon appeared in print, one as a stand-alone document, the two others as articles in the Messenger and Advocate. These lectures were later called the Lectures on Faith.

The Messenger and Advocate issue that carried two of the lectures also announced a change in name and focus for the book being prepared by Joseph’s committee. “These lectures,” it explained, “are being compiled and arranged with other documents of instruction and regulation for the church, titled ‘Doctrine and Covenants of the church of the Latter Day Saints.’”

The original publication of Joseph Smith’s revelations was titled A Book of Commandments for the Government of the Church of Christ.The title of the new book reflected both a change in the Church’s name and a change in the volume’s contents. The Church’s original name, the Church of Christ, had been changed in 1834 to the Church of the Latter Day Saints in order to distinguish it from other churches with similar names. In 1838, a revelation to Joseph Smith would lead to the Church being officially named The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints.

The change in the volume’s contents reflected the book committee’s first assignment to arrange items of doctrine and its subsequent decision to include “other documents of instruction and regulation for the church.” Consequently, when the committee members organized the book, they divided it into two parts. As Joseph Smith and his fellow committee members noted in a preface to the volume, “The first part of the book will be found to contain a series of lectures as delivered before a Theological class in this place, and in consequence of their embracing the important doctrine of salvation, we have arranged them into the following work.”
Thus, the first part of the volume, which fulfilled the assignment to arrange items of doctrine, contained the seven lectures “on the doctrine of the church”—the Lectures on Faith.

“The second part,” wrote Joseph’s committee, “contains items or principles for the regulation of the church, as taken from the revelations which have been given since its organization”—that is, since April 6, 1830—“as well as the former ones”—those given before that date. Hence, the latter part of the book was headed, “Part Second. Covenants and Commandments of the Lord.”

The lectures “on the doctrine,” combined with the “covenants and commandments,” explained the book’s new title: Doctrine and Covenants. The inclusion of the lectures on Church doctrine would create a stir among Church members who resisted the idea of the Church having any kind of creed. Joseph and his fellow committee members anticipated this objection in their preface:

“There may be an aversion in the minds of some against receiving any thing purporting to be articles of religious faith, in consequence of there being so many now extant; but if men believe a system, and profess that it was given by inspiration, certainly, the more intelligibly they can present it, the better. It does not make a principle untrue to print it, neither does it make it true not to print it. . . .
“We have, therefore,” they wrote, “endeavored to present, though in few words, our belief, and when we say this, humbly trust, the faith and principles of this society as a body.”

On August 17, 1835, a Church general assembly convened “to take into consideration the labors of” Joseph’s writing committee that had been charged with compiling the book. In the afternoon of the conference—Joseph Smith and Frederick G. Williams being out of town at the time—the other two committee members, Oliver Cowdery and Sidney Rigdon, became responsible for presenting the new volume to those assembled and obtaining their views of it. One by one, “the several authorities, and the general assembly, by a unanimous vote, accepted of the labors of the committee” and testified to the book’s truth.
With that vote, the Doctrine and Covenants became the Church’s third standard work, along with the Bible and the Book of Mormon.

The 1835 Doctrine and Covenants differed from the 1833 Book of Commandments in several ways. First, it included the text of the seven lectures on Church doctrine. Second, it included more of Joseph Smith’s revelations. Because the Book of Commandments project had been disrupted, not all of the revelations scheduled for inclusion in that volume had been printed. Some revelations from the same time period as those in the Book of Commandments but deliberately left out of it were now added to the Doctrine and Covenants. Other revelations received after the time period of those in the Book of Commandments were also included in the new volume.

The Book of Commandments as actually printed had just 65 “chapters,” the last of which was incomplete. The 1835 Doctrine and Covenants had 103 “sections,” as well as revised minutes of the August 17, 1835, general assembly. It also included a preface, an index, a table of contents, and a list of corrections.

In addition to these differences, the 1835 Doctrine and Covenants included editorial changes, most of them copyediting corrections that did not change the substance of the revelations. Some, however, were substantive updates of the revelations to reflect “changes in church government, structure, and doctrine that had occurred since the time the revelations were first dictated.” As one commentary on the revelations concluded, “If the printed revelations were truly to be ‘a law unto the church, and a rule of faith and practice unto the same,’ only a book with the latest instruction on church government and practice would be useful. The Doctrine and Covenants was intended as a living handbook, containing up-to-date instruction.”

In the weeks following the 1835 general assembly, bound copies of the Doctrine and Covenants at last became available to members of the Church. They sold at a price of one dollar. Although many of the Saints had been waiting eagerly for the revelations to be published, sales were perhaps not as brisk as originally anticipated. That may have been in part because of the poverty of the Saints, the sacrifice of resources for the construction of the Kirtland temple, and the economic turmoil and apostasy that characterized the next two years of the Church’s history in the region. Unsold copies likely turned to ashes when the print shop burned down in 1838.

For those who took advantage of the opportunity to obtain a copy of the volume, however, the blessings were rich. William W. Phelps, who saw his first efforts to print the revelations upset by a mob, took up the subject of the Doctrine and Covenants in the August 1835 issue of the Messenger and Advocate. He wrote:

“Since the commencement of this work, even in Missouri, where, just before it was ready to come out, it fell into the hands of a mob, our anxiety and exertions have been unabated, to put [it] in the possession of the Saints, and all who feel a desire to be saved. We have greatly desired that this little sacred volume might go into the world, that the inhabitants thereof might know what the Lord was about to do in the last days. We know that the word of the Lord is sure, and never fails, and, as faithful servants to him, living in the hope of a glorious resurrection with the just when the righteous arise to meet him in the cloud, we bear testimony that the revelations therein are true and faithful.”
